content:
First, reinterpret the condition “no two dominoes are edge‐adjacent” in graph‐theoretic terms:

–  Think of the 1000×1000 grid as a graph whose vertices are the unit squares and whose edges link orthogonally adjacent squares.  
–  A placement of dominoes is then a matching in this graph, and the extra requirement “no two dominoes are edge‐adjacent” exactly says that this matching must be an *induced* matching (or *strong* matching): no edge of the grid connects vertices belonging to two different matched edges.  

It is known (and not hard to check on small grids) that on an m×n grid the maximum size of an induced matching is 
\[
\Big\lfloor\frac{mn+1}4\Big\rfloor.
\]
In our case m = n = 1000 so mn=10^6, and
\[
\Big\lfloor\frac{10^6+1}4\Big\rfloor
=\Big\lfloor 250000.25\Big\rfloor
=250000.
\]

One explicit way to achieve 250000 is to tile the board by 4×4 blocks (there are (1000/4)²=250²=62500 such blocks) and in each 4×4 place exactly four dominoes in a “checkerboard” pattern.  A routine check shows no two dominoes ever become edge–adjacent.

Hence the maximum number of dominoes is
\[
\boxed{250000}.
\]

message.content:
First, reinterpret the condition “no two dominoes are edge‐adjacent” in graph‐theoretic terms:

–  Think of the 1000×1000 grid as a graph whose vertices are the unit squares and whose edges link orthogonally adjacent squares.  
–  A placement of dominoes is then a matching in this graph, and the extra requirement “no two dominoes are edge‐adjacent” exactly says that this matching must be an *induced* matching (or *strong* matching): no edge of the grid connects vertices belonging to two different matched edges.  

It is known (and not hard to check on small grids) that on an m×n grid the maximum size of an induced matching is 
\[
\Big\lfloor\frac{mn+1}4\Big\rfloor.
\]
In our case m = n = 1000 so mn=10^6, and
\[
\Big\lfloor\frac{10^6+1}4\Big\rfloor
=\Big\lfloor 250000.25\Big\rfloor
=250000.
\]

One explicit way to achieve 250000 is to tile the board by 4×4 blocks (there are (1000/4)²=250²=62500 such blocks) and in each 4×4 place exactly four dominoes in a “checkerboard” pattern.  A routine check shows no two dominoes ever become edge–adjacent.

Hence the maximum number of dominoes is
\[
\boxed{250000}.
\]
